narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your academic journey, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 28.50, upper-middle family income background, and your plan to attend full-time, along with your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 2nd and 1st respectively—both notably lower than the typical student's 6th decile or higher. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 63.4% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged most strongly on your low Year 3 decile ranking, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, since students in higher deciles typically show stronger trajectories toward passing. Your similarly low Year 1 decile ranking reinforced this concern, acting as the second-most influential element in the same direction, highlighting early academic struggles compared to peers. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income provided some counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly toward success as it aligns with resources that have helped similar students overcome hurdles. While your full-time enrollment plan is the norm for most applicants and didn't sway things much either way, these top three factors—dominated by your decile performances—collectively outweighed other details like your LSAT and GPA in driving the no-go decision. In essence, the model saw your grades placing you in the bottom ranks relative to the average applicant, signaling higher risk despite your personal strengths and background. This doesn't define your potential, especially as a determined Hispanic student navigating these paths. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 37 average, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from successful Hispanic lawyers who passed the bar. Building a clearer upward trend in your academics will better position you for reconsideration at this or other schools. Keep pushing—you've got the drive to rewrite this story.
